questions.question: What is the MVC pattern and how does ASP.NET Core implement it?
questions.answer:
MVC (Model-View-Controller) separates an application into three components:

  • Model — data and business logic. In ASP.NET Core: your domain entities, ViewModels, DbContext. Knows nothing about how data is displayed.
  • View — UI presentation. In ASP.NET Core: Razor .cshtml files that render HTML. Only displays data from the ViewModel.
  • Controller — orchestrator. Receives HTTP requests, calls services/repositories, creates a ViewModel, passes it to the View. No business logic in controllers.

Request lifecycle in ASP.NET Core MVC:

  1. Request arrives → Routing matches URL to controller+action
  2. Action filters run (auth, model validation)
  3. Controller action executes: calls services, builds ViewModel
  4. Returns View(viewModel) → Razor renders HTML
  5. Response sent to browser
public class CategoryController : Controller
{
    private readonly IQuestionService _svc;

    [HttpGet]
    public async Task<IActionResult> Detail(string id)
    {
        var questions = await _svc.GetQuestionsByCategoryAsync(id);
        var vm = new CategoryDetailViewModel { Questions = questions, CategoryId = id };
        return View(vm);  // renders Category/Detail.cshtml
    }
}

followUps.question: What is a ViewModel and why use it over passing domain models to views?
followUps.answer: A ViewModel is a class designed specifically for what a view needs to display — it's a projection tailored to one view's requirements. Reasons to use ViewModels instead of domain entities: (1) Security — domain models may have sensitive fields (password hash, internal flags) you don't want rendered in HTML. (2) Over-posting prevention — if a user POSTs back a domain model, they could set fields like IsAdmin = true that weren't in the form. (3) Multiple data sources — a ViewModel can combine Portfolio + Holdings + UserProgress from multiple sources into one flat object. (4) Display formatting — a ViewModel can have FormattedDate, IsPositive, and other display-specific properties the domain model doesn't have.

followUps.question: How does MVC differ from Razor Pages?
followUps.answer: MVC separates routes across Controllers (which can handle many different pages). One controller may have actions for list, detail, create, edit — different pages sharing one controller class. Razor Pages is page-centric — each .cshtml file is a self-contained page with its own PageModel class handling GET and POST directly via OnGet() and OnPost() handlers. Razor Pages reduces the indirection of controllers: the page, its model, and its handler are co-located. Microsoft recommends Razor Pages for new server-rendered web apps because the page-centric model maps more naturally to how web UIs are actually structured. MVC is preferred when you need API endpoints alongside views in the same app, or when the team is already familiar with MVC.

questions.tip: Fat Controller anti-pattern: Controllers should be thin orchestrators — receive request, call service, return result. Business logic in controllers is untestable and violates SRP. Move all business logic to services.

questions.question: What is a ViewModel and why should you use it instead of domain models in views?
questions.answer:
A ViewModel is a class designed specifically for what a view needs to display — it's a projection of your domain model tailored to the view's requirements.

Why not pass domain models directly to views?

  • Views often need data from multiple sources — ViewModel can combine Portfolio + Holdings + UserProgress into one flat object
  • Security — domain models may have sensitive fields (password hash, internal flags) you don't want rendered in HTML
  • Over-posting vulnerability — if the user can POST back a domain model, they could set fields you didn't intend (e.g., IsAdmin = true)
  • Views may need display-specific properties — formatted date strings, computed display values
// Domain model (EF Core entity)
public class Portfolio
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string UserId { get; set; } = string.Empty; // don't expose
    public decimal InternalCalculatedValue { get; set; } // sensitive
    // ...
}

// ViewModel — only what the view needs
public class PortfolioSummaryViewModel
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    public string FormattedValue { get; set; } = string.Empty;  // "₹1,23,456"
    public string ChangePercent { get; set; } = string.Empty;   // "+2.3%"
    public bool IsPositive { get; set; }  // for CSS class selection
}

followUps.question: What is AutoMapper and when should you use it?
followUps.answer: AutoMapper is a library that maps properties from one object to another by convention (matching property names). Instead of manually writing vm.Name = entity.Name; vm.Id = entity.Id; for each property, you configure a mapping profile once and call _mapper.Map<PortfolioViewModel>(portfolio). Use it when: you have many simple property-to-property mappings, DTOs mirror domain objects closely, and you're tired of repetitive manual mapping code. Avoid it when: mappings are complex with significant transformation logic (that logic becomes invisible in AutoMapper configuration), or for very few simple DTOs where manual mapping is clearer. My opinion: for straightforward CRUD apps AutoMapper is a convenience; for domain-rich apps, explicit manual mapping or record-based projections via LINQ Select are clearer.

followUps.question: What is the Input Model vs ViewModel distinction?
followUps.answer: A ViewModel carries data from the server to the view for display (GET). An Input Model (or Request DTO) carries data from the view to the server on form POST. They're often different: the ViewModel may include computed display fields and related data the input model doesn't need. The input model contains exactly the fields the user fills in. Keeping them separate prevents over-posting attacks — even if an attacker adds extra fields to the POST body, the action only binds the Input Model's declared properties. Example: PortfolioViewModel for display; CreatePortfolioInputModel for the create form POST, with only Name and Description fields.

questions.tip: Over-posting attack: If you bind a POST directly to a domain model, a malicious user can set any public property by including it in the form. Use [Bind("Name,Description")] or separate input models (DTOs) for POST/PUT actions.

questions.question: What is TempData and when do you use it?
questions.answer:
TempData stores data that survives exactly one redirect. After the data is read, it's automatically deleted. Used for the Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) pattern — pass a success/error message from a POST action to the GET action it redirects to.

// POST action — after saving, redirect
[HttpPost]
[ValidateAntiForgeryToken]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create(CreatePortfolioViewModel model)
{
    if (!ModelState.IsValid) return View(model);

    await _service.CreateAsync(model);
    TempData["SuccessMessage"] = "Portfolio created successfully!";
    return RedirectToAction(nameof(Index));  // PRG: redirect to GET
}

// GET action — reads and displays the message
public async Task<IActionResult> Index()
{
    ViewBag.Success = TempData["SuccessMessage"];  // TempData auto-cleared after read
    var portfolios = await _service.GetAllAsync();
    return View(portfolios);
}

// In Razor view
@if (ViewBag.Success != null)
{
    <div class="alert alert-success">@ViewBag.Success</div>
}

TempData storage: By default stored in the session. Can use cookies with CookieTempDataProvider.

followUps.question: What is TempData.Keep() and Peek()?
followUps.answer: By default, reading TempData marks it for deletion — it's gone after the response. TempData.Keep("key") marks the value to survive the next request as well (prevents deletion after read). TempData.Peek("key") reads the value without marking it for deletion — useful when you need to check the value without consuming it, so it's still available for the actual display. Use case: a multi-step redirect where TempData needs to survive more than one hop. For most scenarios, the default auto-delete behavior is what you want — use Keep/Peek sparingly as they make the lifecycle harder to reason about.

followUps.question: TempData vs ViewBag vs ViewData?
followUps.answer: ViewData is a ViewDataDictionary that carries data from controller to view within the same request. Requires casting on the view side. ViewBag is a dynamic wrapper around ViewData — same lifetime, cleaner syntax. Both survive only the current request and are available only to the view (not across redirects). TempData persists across exactly one redirect — it uses session or cookies for storage. Use ViewData/ViewBag for passing small extra data alongside a ViewModel to the view. Use TempData for cross-redirect messaging (PRG pattern). Best practice: prefer strongly-typed ViewModels over ViewBag for main data; use TempData only for transient messages like success/error notifications.

questions.tip: PRG pattern is important for browser UX — after a POST, redirecting to GET prevents "Form resubmission" warnings when the user refreshes. Always redirect after a successful POST; use TempData to carry the success message across.

questions.question: What are Tag Helpers in ASP.NET Core Razor views?
questions.answer:
Tag Helpers are server-side components that participate in rendering HTML elements. They replace the older Html Helper syntax with a more HTML-friendly syntax.

<!-- Old Html Helper syntax -->
@Html.ActionLink("Details", "Detail", "Question", new { id = q.Id }, null)
@Html.BeginForm("Create", "Portfolio", FormMethod.Post)

<!-- Tag Helper syntax (cleaner, reads like HTML) -->
<a asp-controller="Question" asp-action="Detail" asp-route-id="@q.Id">Details</a>
<form asp-controller="Portfolio" asp-action="Create" method="post">

<!-- Form elements with model binding -->
<input asp-for="Name" class="form-control" />
<span asp-validation-for="Name" class="text-danger"></span>
<label asp-for="Name"></label>

<!-- Environment-specific content -->
<environment include="Development">
    <script src="~/js/site.js"></script>
</environment>
<environment exclude="Development">
    <script src="~/js/site.min.js" asp-append-version="true"></script>
</environment>

asp-append-version="true" adds a cache-busting hash to the URL — crucial for forcing browsers to reload updated CSS/JS files after deployment.

followUps.question: How do you create a custom Tag Helper?
followUps.answer: Extend TagHelper and override Process or ProcessAsync. The class name (minus "TagHelper") determines the HTML element it targets. Use [HtmlTargetElement] to specify the element and required attributes. Example: public class AlertTagHelper : TagHelper targets <alert> elements. Decorate properties with [HtmlAttributeName("type")] to bind HTML attributes. In Process, modify the TagHelperOutput to set element name, attributes, and content. Register in _ViewImports.cshtml: @addTagHelper *, YourAssemblyName. Custom Tag Helpers are great for reusable alert boxes, badge components, or conditional rendering that would otherwise require messy Razor inline C#.

followUps.question: What is asp-append-version?
followUps.answer: asp-append-version="true" on a <script>, <link>, or <img> element appends a query string hash based on the file's content: site.css?v=ABC123DEF. This hash changes whenever the file's content changes, forcing browsers and CDNs to download the new version instead of serving the cached old version. Without cache busting, deploying a CSS update may show the old styles for hours or days for users with cached files. The hash is computed at startup and cached — no performance hit per request. Always use asp-append-version on static assets that are updated between deployments.

questions.tip: asp-append-version appends a content hash to the URL: site.css?v=abc123. This busts the CDN/browser cache whenever the file changes, without requiring you to manually update version numbers. Use it on all static asset references.

questions.question: What are partial views and view components? When do you use each?
questions.answer:
Both are reusable view units, but they serve different purposes:

Partial Views — share the rendering context with the parent view. Same ViewData, same model type. Used for breaking large views into smaller files. Synchronous by default (no async support in _PartialName.cshtml).

<!-- Render partial -->
<partial name="_QuestionCard" model="question" />
<!-- Or in controller: return PartialView("_QuestionCard", question) -->

View Components — independent mini-controllers with their own logic and view. Can query the database, inject services, run async code. Used for reusable UI widgets with their own data requirements (sidebar, cart summary, notification bell).

// View Component class
public class CategoryProgressViewComponent : ViewComponent
{
    private readonly IProgressService _svc;
    public CategoryProgressViewComponent(IProgressService svc) => _svc = svc;

    public async Task<IViewComponentResult> InvokeAsync(string categoryId)
    {
        var userId = HttpContext.User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier)!;
        var progress = await _svc.GetCategoryProgressAsync(userId, categoryId);
        return View(progress);
    }
}

// In Razor view
@await Component.InvokeAsync("CategoryProgress", new { categoryId = "di" })

followUps.question: What is the difference between partial view and layout?
followUps.answer: A layout (_Layout.cshtml) is the master page template that wraps other views — it provides the outer HTML structure (header, navigation, footer) and calls @RenderBody() where child view content is inserted. One layout wraps many views. A partial view is a reusable fragment rendered inside a view — it's not a wrapper but an embedded component. The layout is automatically applied to views via _ViewStart.cshtml's Layout = "_Layout". Partial views are explicitly included with <partial name="..." />. Layouts define the page shell; partial views define reusable inner content like cards, modals, or repeated sections.

followUps.question: When would you use a View Component vs AJAX?
followUps.answer: Use a View Component when you need server-side rendering of a self-contained widget with its own data — the widget renders with the initial page load and users don't need to interact with it independently. Example: a sidebar showing category progress. Use AJAX when the widget needs to update dynamically without a full page reload — the user clicks something and the content changes. Example: live search results, real-time progress updates, infinite scroll. View Components render synchronously with the page (good for SEO and initial load time). AJAX adds interactivity but requires JavaScript and handling loading states. Often combined: initial render via View Component, subsequent updates via AJAX returning a partial view.

questions.tip: Use View Components for server-rendered widgets that need their own data. Use partial views for splitting large views into manageable pieces that share the parent's model. If the widget needs independent async data loading, it must be a View Component — partial views can't run async code independently.

questions.question: What is the difference between MVC and MVVM?
questions.answer:
Both are architectural patterns that separate UI from business logic, but they differ in how the View and logic layer interact.

MVC (Model-View-Controller) — used in ASP.NET Core MVC:

  • Model — data and business logic
  • View — Razor template that renders HTML (passive — just displays what it's given)
  • Controller — receives HTTP requests, calls services, builds a ViewModel, selects and returns the View
  • Flow: Request → Controller → Model/Services → Controller builds ViewModel → View renders HTML → Response
// Controller drives everything — picks the View and passes data
public class QuestionController : Controller
{
    public async Task<IActionResult> Detail(string id)
    {
        var question = await _questionService.GetByIdAsync(id);
        var vm = new QuestionDetailViewModel { Question = question };
        return View(vm); // controller selects which view to render
    }
}

MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) — used in Angular, Blazor, WPF:

  • Model — data (API response DTOs, domain objects)
  • ViewModel — TypeScript/C# class that exposes state and commands; contains all UI logic
  • View — template that binds directly to the ViewModel; no code-behind logic
  • No controller — View and ViewModel are connected via two-way data binding
// Angular component IS the ViewModel — View binds to it
export class DashboardComponent {
  portfolios = signal<Portfolio[]>([]);  // View binds: {{ portfolios() }}
  isLoading = signal(false);            // View binds: [disabled]="isLoading()"
  totalValue = computed(() =>           // Derived state, auto-updates
    this.portfolios().reduce((s, p) => s + p.value, 0));
}

followUps.question: Does ASP.NET Core MVC use ViewModels like MVVM?
followUps.answer: The ViewModel concept in ASP.NET Core MVC is borrowed from MVVM. In ASP.NET Core, ViewModels are POCOs built specifically for a single view — they aggregate data from multiple domain objects into exactly what that view needs. Example: QuestionDetailViewModel combines question data + user progress + notes into one object. However, this is not true MVVM because there is no two-way data binding — the Controller still builds the ViewModel and passes it one-way to the Razor View. The View cannot update the ViewModel without a full HTTP form POST. True MVVM requires a data-binding framework. ASP.NET Core Blazor is closer to MVVM: the component class acts as a ViewModel with @bind two-way bindings in the template. In your projects: InterviewPrepPortal uses MVC; FolioTrack's Angular frontend uses MVVM.

followUps.question: What is MVP (Model-View-Presenter)?
followUps.answer: MVP is a variant of MVC used in older Windows Forms and Android. The Presenter contains all UI logic and holds a reference to an IView interface — making it fully testable without the real UI. Unlike MVC (Controller doesn't hold a reference to the View), in MVP the Presenter directly calls methods on the IView interface. Unlike MVVM (binding is automatic), in MVP the Presenter explicitly updates the View. Rarely used in modern ASP.NET development — MVC covers server-rendered apps and MVVM covers SPAs. MVP is relevant mainly for legacy desktop apps or when you need fully automated UI testing without a binding framework.

questions.tip: Key difference: In MVC the Controller selects and pushes to the View. In MVVM the View pulls from and binds to the ViewModel automatically. ASP.NET Core = MVC. Angular/Blazor = MVVM. Your InterviewPrepPortal is MVC; your FolioTrack Angular frontend is MVVM.

questions.question: What is the difference between .NET Framework and .NET Core / .NET 5+?
questions.answer:
This is one of the most common .NET screening questions.

.NET Framework.NET Core / .NET 5+
Windows-onlyCross-platform (Windows, Linux, macOS)
Machine-wide install (GAC)Side-by-side installs, self-contained deployments
Slow release cycle (tied to Windows updates)Fast annual releases (.NET 6, 7, 8, 9…)
Monolithic — everything includedModular NuGet packages, smaller footprint
IIS required for hostingBuilt-in Kestrel, runs anywhere (Docker, Lambda, Windows Service)
Web.config (XML)appsettings.json + environment variables
Global.asax + Startup.cs (two classes)Single Program.cs (top-level statements, .NET 6+)
Last version: 4.8.1 — maintenance onlyCurrent: .NET 9. LTS: .NET 8

Timeline: .NET Core 1.0 (2016) → 2.x, 3.x → .NET 5 (dropped "Core" name, unified platform) → .NET 6 LTS → .NET 7 → .NET 8 LTS → .NET 9.

All your projects use unified .NET: InterviewPrepPortal (.NET 8), FolioTrack (.NET 10), CS_PM_Service (.NET 6).

followUps.question: What is .NET Standard?
followUps.answer: .NET Standard is a specification (not an implementation) defining a common API set that all .NET implementations (.NET Framework, .NET Core, Xamarin, Unity) must support. A class library targeting .NET Standard 2.0 runs on .NET Framework 4.6.1+ and .NET Core 2.0+ without modification — solving the cross-platform library compatibility problem. With .NET 5+ (unified .NET), .NET Standard is largely obsolete — there is now one platform. New libraries should target net8.0 directly. Only target .NET Standard 2.0 if your library must support the old .NET Framework 4.x simultaneously — e.g., a shared utility library used by both a legacy .NET Framework monolith and a new .NET 8 microservice.

followUps.question: What changed in Program.cs with .NET 6?
followUps.answer: .NET 6 introduced the minimal hosting model. The old pattern required two classes: Program (with CreateHostBuilder) and Startup (with ConfigureServices and Configure methods). .NET 6 merged both into a single Program.cs using top-level statements and WebApplication.CreateBuilder(): register services via builder.Services, build the app with builder.Build(), configure middleware on app, then call app.Run(). No class declaration, no Main method. Less boilerplate, single file, more readable. The Startup class still works for backward compatibility, but all new .NET 6+ projects should use the minimal hosting model. InterviewPrepPortal uses this pattern.

questions.tip: For interviews: emphasise cross-platform support, performance (Kestrel + Native AOT), Docker-ready, and the unified .NET 5+ story. .NET Framework 4.x is in maintenance mode — no new features. Target .NET 8 LTS for new projects. Know the key difference in Program.cs between pre-.NET 6 (Startup class) and .NET 6+ (minimal hosting).

questions.question: What is the difference between Session and Cookie in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Both persist state across stateless HTTP requests but differ in where data lives.

SessionCookie
Data stored server-sideData stored in the browser
Browser holds only a session IDFull data travels in every request header
Secure — sensitive data never leaves serverVisible to browser (unless HttpOnly)
Default: 20 min sliding expiryConfigurable — session or persistent
Lost on server restart (in-memory default)Persists across server restarts
No size limit (server memory)4KB per cookie limit
// Session setup — Program.cs
builder.Services.AddSession(options => {
    options.IdleTimeout = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(30);
    options.Cookie.HttpOnly = true;    // not accessible via JS
    options.Cookie.IsEssential = true; // required for GDPR
});
app.UseSession(); // must come before MapControllers

// Using session in a controller
HttpContext.Session.SetString("UserId", user.Id.ToString());
var userId = HttpContext.Session.GetString("UserId");

// Writing a cookie
Response.Cookies.Append("Theme", "dark", new CookieOptions {
    HttpOnly = false,  // JS-readable (for theming)
    Secure   = true,   // HTTPS only
    SameSite = SameSiteMode.Strict,
    Expires  = DateTimeOffset.UtcNow.AddDays(30)
});
// Reading a cookie
var theme = Request.Cookies["Theme"];

followUps.question: What is the default session storage in ASP.NET Core and how do you scale it?
followUps.answer: By default, ASP.NET Core stores session data in-memory (IMemoryCache) — data is lost on application restart and cannot be shared across multiple server instances. For production scale-out, replace with a distributed cache: Redis — builder.Services.AddStackExchangeRedisCache(o => o.Configuration = "redis:6379"); builder.Services.AddSession(); — or SQL Server via AddDistributedSqlServerCache(). The session cookie name defaults to .AspNetCore.Session. The 20-minute timeout is a sliding expiration — every request resets it. If no request arrives within 20 minutes the session is abandoned and purged from cache. In-memory session also fails in multi-instance deployments (load balancer sends requests to different servers) — this is the most common session bug in production.

followUps.question: What are HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite cookie flags?
followUps.answer: HttpOnly — prevents document.cookie JavaScript access. Blocks XSS attacks from stealing the cookie value. Always set for auth cookies and session ID cookies. Secure — cookie is only sent over HTTPS. Prevents theft via HTTP man-in-the-middle. Always set in production. SameSite — controls cross-origin sending: Strict (never sent cross-origin — best security, prevents CSRF), Lax (sent on top-level navigation GETs only — default since Chrome 80), None (always sent, requires Secure=true — needed for cross-origin use cases like OAuth flows). ASP.NET Core Identity sets all three (HttpOnly + Secure + Strict) on its auth cookie by default. If storing a JWT in a cookie, always use HttpOnly + Secure to prevent XSS theft.

questions.tip: Decision rule: Session = temporary server-side state within a visit (wizard steps, cart). Cookie = lightweight browser-side preference that survives visits (theme, language). Auth = JWT in an HttpOnly cookie (NOT localStorage — XSS can steal localStorage). Default ASP.NET Core session timeout = 20 minutes sliding.

questions.question: What is the ASP.NET Core MVC request lifecycle / pipeline?
questions.answer:
Understanding the full request lifecycle is important for debugging and for knowing where to inject custom behaviour.

High-level flow:

  1. HTTP Request arrives at Kestrel (or IIS) and enters the ASP.NET Core middleware pipeline.
  2. Middleware pipeline runs — each Use() middleware executes in order: authentication, authorisation, routing, CORS, static files, etc.
  3. Routing middleware matches the URL to a controller/action and stores the route data.
  4. Controller instantiation — the framework creates the controller using DI to inject constructor dependencies.
  5. Authorization filters — IAuthorizationFilter.OnAuthorization runs. If denied, short-circuits here.
  6. Resource filters — OnResourceExecuting runs (before model binding).
  7. Model binding — values from route, query string, form, and body are bound to action parameters.
  8. Model validation — data annotation validators run; ModelState.IsValid is set.
  9. Action filters — OnActionExecuting runs before the action method.
  10. Action method executes — your controller code runs and returns an IActionResult.
  11. Action filters — OnActionExecuted runs after the action.
  12. Result filters — OnResultExecuting runs before the result is processed.
  13. View rendering (for ViewResult) — Razor template is compiled and HTML is produced.
  14. Result filters — OnResultExecuted runs.
  15. Exception filters — catch unhandled exceptions anywhere in the filter pipeline.
  16. Response travels back through the middleware pipeline and is sent to the client.

followUps.question: What is the difference between middleware and filters in ASP.NET Core?
followUps.answer:
Middleware operates on the raw HTTP pipeline — it sees HttpContext and runs for every request, regardless of whether MVC handles it. Use middleware for cross-cutting concerns that apply broadly: authentication, logging, HTTPS redirection, static files.

Filters operate within the MVC layer — they have access to MVC abstractions like ActionContext, ModelState, and controller metadata. They only run for requests that route to MVC controllers/actions. Use filters for MVC-specific concerns: caching action results, validating ModelState, auditing specific controller actions.

Order: Middleware runs before filters. If a middleware short-circuits (returns a response), filters never run.

questions.tip: Order matters: Authorization filters run before action filters. If you put authorisation logic in an action filter, it runs after action filters — meaning other action filters have already run. Always use authorization filters (or middleware) for security checks.

questions.question: What is the difference between ViewBag, ViewData, and TempData in ASP.NET Core MVC?
questions.answer:
All three pass data from a controller to a view, but they differ in scope, type safety, and lifetime.

ViewData

  • Type: ViewDataDictionary (a Dictionary<string, object>)
  • Requires casting when reading values in the view.
  • Lifetime: current request only.
// Controller
ViewData["Title"] = "My Page";
ViewData["Count"] = 42;

// View
<h1>@ViewData["Title"]</h1>
<p>@((int)ViewData["Count"] + 1)</p> // cast required

ViewBag

  • Type: dynamic wrapper over ViewData — they share the same underlying dictionary.
  • No casting needed — dynamic resolution at runtime.
  • Lifetime: current request only.
  • No IntelliSense support.
// Controller
ViewBag.Title = "My Page"; // same as ViewData["Title"]
ViewBag.Count = 42;

// View — no cast needed
<h1>@ViewBag.Title</h1>
<p>@(ViewBag.Count + 1)</p>

TempData

  • Type: ITempDataDictionary
  • Lifetime: survives one redirect — data persists until it is read, then it is automatically deleted.
  • Stored in session or cookie (configurable).
  • Primarily used to pass messages after a POST-Redirect-GET flow.
// Controller — POST action
TempData["SuccessMessage"] = "Record saved!";
return RedirectToAction("Index"); // survives the redirect

// Controller — GET action (after redirect)
// View
@if (TempData["SuccessMessage"] != null)
{
    <div class="alert">@TempData["SuccessMessage"]</div>
}

followUps.question: What is the recommended approach — ViewBag/ViewData or a strongly-typed ViewModel?
followUps.answer:
Always prefer a strongly-typed ViewModel over ViewBag/ViewData for main page data. Reasons:

  • Compile-time safety — typos in property names are caught at compile time, not at runtime.
  • IntelliSense — IDE autocompletion works on ViewModel properties.
  • Refactoring — renaming a property in a ViewModel updates all usages. Renaming a ViewBag string key doesn't.
  • Clarity — the View's @model MyViewModel declaration explicitly documents what data the view expects.

ViewBag/ViewData are acceptable for small ancillary data that doesn't warrant a dedicated ViewModel property (e.g., page title in a layout). For the main data of a view, use a ViewModel.

questions.tip: Memory trick: ViewBag = ViewData with a hat on (dynamic wrapper). TempData = Temp = temporary across ONE redirect. ViewModel = the proper way.

questions.question: What is the Razor view engine in ASP.NET Core MVC?
questions.answer:
Razor is ASP.NET Core's server-side template engine. It lets you embed C# code directly inside HTML using the @ symbol. Razor files have the .cshtml extension. The engine compiles Razor templates into C# classes at build time (or on first request), which then execute to produce the HTML response.

Key Razor syntax:

<!-- Model declaration — strongly-typed view -->
@model MyApp.ViewModels.ProductViewModel

<!-- Render a model property -->
<h1>@Model.Name</h1>

<!-- C# expression -->
<p>Price: @Model.Price.ToString("C")</p>

<!-- Code block -->
@{
    var discount = Model.Price * 0.1m;
    ViewData["Title"] = Model.Name;
}

<!-- Conditional -->
@if (Model.InStock)
{
    <span class="badge bg-success">In Stock</span>
}
else
{
    <span class="badge bg-danger">Out of Stock</span>
}

<!-- Loop -->
@foreach (var tag in Model.Tags)
{
    <span class="tag">@tag</span>
}

<!-- HTML helper -->
@Html.DisplayFor(m => m.Name)

<!-- Tag helper (modern, preferred) -->
<a asp-controller="Product" asp-action="Details" asp-route-id="@Model.Id">View</a>

Layout files:

_Layout.cshtml defines the shared HTML shell. @RenderBody() marks where child view content is injected. Views specify their layout in _ViewStart.cshtml or inline with @{ Layout = "_Layout"; }.

followUps.question: What is the difference between HTML Helpers and Tag Helpers?
followUps.answer:
HTML Helpers are C# methods that return HTML strings: @Html.TextBoxFor(m => m.Name). They mix C# method calls with HTML, which can be hard to read and doesn't work well with IDE HTML tooling.

Tag Helpers (ASP.NET Core 1.0+) look like regular HTML attributes, making views more natural for front-end developers: <input asp-for="Name" />. They participate in normal HTML IntelliSense and are the preferred approach. Tag helpers are processed server-side and render final HTML to the client. Common tag helpers: asp-for, asp-action, asp-controller, asp-route-*, asp-validation-for.

questions.tip: Razor files are compiled: In production, Razor views are compiled into the assembly during publish (Ahead-of-Time compilation). This means view compilation errors are caught at publish time, not first-request. Runtime compilation (for dev convenience) can be enabled separately.

questions.question: What is the entry point of an ASP.NET Core MVC application? What happens at startup?
questions.answer:
The entry point is Program.cs. In .NET 6+ it uses top-level statements (no explicit Main method visible), but the compiler generates one. Here's the full startup flow:

Program.cs structure:

// 1. Create the builder — configures app host, DI container, config sources
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

// 2. Register services into the DI container
builder.Services.AddControllersWithViews();
builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(opts =>
    opts.UseSqlite(builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("Default")));
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailService, SmtpEmailService>();

// 3. Build the WebApplication (finalises DI container)
var app = builder.Build();

// 4. Configure the middleware pipeline (order matters!)
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
    app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();
else
    app.UseExceptionHandler("/Home/Error");

app.UseHttpsRedirection();
app.UseStaticFiles();     // serve wwwroot files
app.UseRouting();
app.UseAuthentication();  // must be before UseAuthorization
app.UseAuthorization();

// 5. Map endpoints
app.MapControllerRoute(
    name: "default",
    pattern: "{controller=Home}/{action=Index}/{id?}");

// 6. Start the server and listen for requests
app.Run();

Startup sequence summary:

  1. Configuration sources loaded (appsettings.json, environment variables, secrets)
  2. Logging configured
  3. Services registered (DI container built)
  4. Middleware pipeline assembled
  5. Server starts (Kestrel binds to port)
  6. Requests flow through pipeline

followUps.question: What was the Startup.cs class in earlier ASP.NET Core versions?
followUps.answer:
Before .NET 6, ASP.NET Core split startup logic across two places:

  • Program.cs — Main() method, creates and runs the host via CreateHostBuilder().
  • Startup.cs — ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection) for DI registrations, and Configure(IApplicationBuilder) for middleware.

In .NET 6+, the minimal hosting model merged both into a single Program.cs using the WebApplicationBuilder pattern. The Startup.cs approach still works (you can still use it) but it's no longer the default template.

questions.tip: Middleware order is critical: UseRouting must come before UseAuthentication, UseAuthentication before UseAuthorization, and both before MapControllers. Getting the order wrong causes subtle bugs (401s, 404s, or unauthenticated requests reaching controllers).

questions.question: What is Bundling and Minification in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Bundling combines multiple CSS or JavaScript files into a single file, reducing the number of HTTP requests the browser needs to make to load a page.

Minification removes unnecessary characters from CSS/JS files — whitespace, comments, long variable names — to reduce file size and improve download speed.

Why they matter:

  • Without bundling: a page with 10 CSS files makes 10 HTTP requests. With bundling: 1 request.
  • Without minification: app.js might be 200KB. After minification: 80KB.
  • Together they significantly reduce Time-To-First-Byte and page load time.

ASP.NET Core approach — LibMan + bundleconfig.json:

// bundleconfig.json
[
  {
    "outputFileName": "wwwroot/css/bundle.min.css",
    "inputFiles": [
      "wwwroot/css/site.css",
      "wwwroot/lib/bootstrap/css/bootstrap.css"
    ],
    "minify": { "enabled": true }
  },
  {
    "outputFileName": "wwwroot/js/bundle.min.js",
    "inputFiles": [
      "wwwroot/js/site.js",
      "wwwroot/lib/jquery/jquery.js"
    ],
    "minify": { "enabled": true }
  }
]

Environment Tag Helper — dev vs production:

<!-- In _Layout.cshtml -->
<environment include="Development">
    <!-- Individual files for easy debugging -->
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="~/css/site.css" />
</environment>
<environment exclude="Development">
    <!-- Bundled + minified for production -->
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="~/css/bundle.min.css" asp-append-version="true" />
</environment>

asp-append-version="true" appends a hash to bust the browser cache when the file changes.

followUps.question: What tools are commonly used for bundling and minification in modern ASP.NET Core?
followUps.answer:
In modern ASP.NET Core projects you have several options:

  • BuildBundlerMinifier NuGet — processes bundleconfig.json at build time. Lightweight, no Node.js required.
  • Webpack / Vite — full-featured JavaScript module bundlers. Used when the front end is complex (React, Vue, Angular with ASP.NET Core as API backend).
  • LibMan (Library Manager) — client-side library manager built into Visual Studio, downloads CSS/JS packages from CDN/npm.
  • ASP.NET Core SPA integration — for Angular, React SPAs, the front-end build pipeline produces optimised bundles that ASP.NET Core serves as static files.

For a pure MVC (server-rendered) project, BuildBundlerMinifier is the simplest. For SPAs, use Webpack or Vite.

questions.tip: Cache busting: Always use asp-append-version="true" on bundled assets. Without it, a user's browser may cache the old bundle and not see your changes for days.

questions.question: What are the types of routing in ASP.NET Core MVC?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core MVC supports two routing approaches that can be used together.

1. Conventional Routing

Routes are defined centrally in Program.cs using templates. The URL pattern is matched to a controller/action by convention.

app.MapControllerRoute(
    name: "default",
    pattern: "{controller=Home}/{action=Index}/{id?}");

// Custom conventional route
app.MapControllerRoute(
    name: "blog",
    pattern: "blog/{year}/{month}/{slug}",
    defaults: new { controller = "Blog", action = "Post" });

URL /Products/Details/5 maps to ProductsController.Details(5) by convention.

2. Attribute Routing

Routes are defined directly on controllers and actions using attributes. More explicit and flexible.

[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]  // api/products
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]                     // GET api/products
    public IActionResult GetAll() { }

    [HttpGet("{id:int}")]          // GET api/products/5
    public IActionResult GetById(int id) { }

    [HttpGet("search")]            // GET api/products/search
    public IActionResult Search([FromQuery] string q) { }

    [HttpPost]                    // POST api/products
    public IActionResult Create([FromBody] ProductDto dto) { }
}

Route Constraints:

[HttpGet("{id:int:min(1)}")]         // id must be int >= 1
[HttpGet("{name:alpha:length(3,20)}")] // name must be alpha, 3-20 chars
[HttpGet("{date:datetime}")]          // date must be parseable as DateTime

When to use which:

  • Conventional — traditional MVC (server-rendered views), simple CRUD controllers.
  • Attribute — Web API controllers, complex routing, RESTful design, areas.

followUps.question: What are Areas in ASP.NET Core MVC and how do they affect routing?
followUps.answer:
Areas allow you to partition a large MVC app into smaller functional groupings, each with its own controllers, views, and models. Common use: separating Admin area from the main site.

// Register area route BEFORE the default route
app.MapControllerRoute(
    name: "areas",
    pattern: "{area:exists}/{controller=Home}/{action=Index}/{id?}");

// Area controller
[Area("Admin")]
public class DashboardController : Controller
{
    public IActionResult Index() => View();
}
// URL: /Admin/Dashboard/Index

Views go in Areas/Admin/Views/Dashboard/Index.cshtml.

questions.tip: Web API should always use attribute routing. Conventional routing is designed for controller/action/id patterns and doesn't express RESTful resource URLs well. For APIs, [Route] and [HttpGet/Post/etc.] are the standard.

questions.question: What is RenderBody() and RenderSection() in ASP.NET Core Razor layouts?
questions.answer:
These are Razor directives used in layout files (_Layout.cshtml) to control where child view content and named sections are injected.

RenderBody()

Marks the location where the main content of a child view is rendered. Every layout must have exactly one @RenderBody() call.

<!-- _Layout.cshtml -->
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>@ViewData["Title"]</title>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="~/css/site.css" />
    @await RenderSectionAsync("Styles", required: false)
</head>
<body>
    <nav>...navbar...</nav>
    
    <main>
        @RenderBody()  <!-- child view content goes here -->
    </main>
    
    <footer>...</footer>
    
    <script src="~/js/site.js"></script>
    @await RenderSectionAsync("Scripts", required: false)
</body>
</html>

RenderSection() / RenderSectionAsync()

Defines a named placeholder in the layout that child views can optionally fill with additional content (e.g., page-specific CSS or JavaScript).

  • required: true — child views MUST define this section or a runtime error occurs.
  • required: false — the section is optional; if not defined, it's simply empty.
<!-- In a child view (e.g., Index.cshtml) -->
@model ProductListViewModel

<h1>Products</h1>
<!-- Main body content — goes where @RenderBody() is in layout -->
@foreach (var p in Model.Products) { ... }

<!-- Named section — goes where @RenderSection("Scripts") is -->
@section Scripts {
    <script src="~/js/datatables.min.js"></script>
    <script>initDataTable('#products-table');</script>
}

followUps.question: What is _ViewStart.cshtml and what is it used for?
followUps.answer:
_ViewStart.cshtml is a special file that runs before every view in the same directory (and subdirectories). Its primary use is to set the default layout so you don't have to specify it in every view.

<!-- Views/_ViewStart.cshtml -->
@{
    Layout = "_Layout";
}

A view can override this by setting its own @{ Layout = "_OtherLayout"; } or setting Layout = null to render without any layout. _ViewImports.cshtml is a related file that sets global @using directives and @addTagHelper statements so they apply to all views in the folder.

questions.tip: Sections = injection points: Think of RenderBody as 'main slot' and RenderSection as named slots in a layout. Scripts section is the most common — it lets child views inject page-specific JavaScript at the bottom of the layout without touching the layout file.

questions.question: What is the default session timeout in ASP.NET Core and how do you configure it?
questions.answer:
The default session idle timeout in ASP.NET Core is 20 minutes. After 20 minutes of inactivity (no requests), the session expires and its data is cleared.

Configuring session in Program.cs:

// 1. Add session services
builder.Services.AddDistributedMemoryCache(); // or Redis for production
builder.Services.AddSession(options =>
{
    options.IdleTimeout = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(30); // custom timeout
    options.Cookie.HttpOnly = true;  // not accessible via JS
    options.Cookie.IsEssential = true; // needed for GDPR compliance
    options.Cookie.SecurePolicy = CookieSecurePolicy.Always; // HTTPS only
    options.Cookie.Name = ".MyApp.Session";
});

// 2. Add the middleware (must be between UseRouting and MapControllers)
app.UseRouting();
app.UseSession();  // <-- must come before MapControllers
app.MapControllerRoute(...);

Using session in a controller:

// Set a value
HttpContext.Session.SetString("UserId", userId.ToString());
HttpContext.Session.SetInt32("CartCount", 3);

// Get a value
string userId = HttpContext.Session.GetString("UserId");
int? count = HttpContext.Session.GetInt32("CartCount");

// Remove a key
HttpContext.Session.Remove("CartCount");

// Clear all session data
HttpContext.Session.Clear();

Session vs Cookie lifetime:

The session IdleTimeout is the inactivity timeout. The session cookie itself (by default) is a session cookie — it's deleted when the browser closes. To make the session survive browser restarts, you'd set a persistent cookie duration, but that's uncommon for server-side sessions.

followUps.question: How do you use Redis as a distributed session store in production?
followUps.answer:
In-memory session (AddDistributedMemoryCache) is only suitable for single-server deployments. In a load-balanced environment, each server has its own memory — a user may hit a different server on the next request and lose their session. Use Redis for a shared session store:

// Install: Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.StackExchangeRedis
builder.Services.AddStackExchangeRedisCache(options =>
{
    options.Configuration = builder.Configuration["Redis:ConnectionString"];
    options.InstanceName = "MyApp:";
});

builder.Services.AddSession(options =>
{
    options.IdleTimeout = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(30);
});

All servers now read/write session from the shared Redis instance. Any server can serve any user's request.

questions.tip: Session vs Cookie: Session stores data server-side (referenced by a cookie ID). Cookies store data client-side (sent on every request). For sensitive data, use session. For persistent preferences (theme, language), use cookies.

questions.question: What are Data Annotations in ASP.NET Core? How do you use them for validation?
questions.answer:
Data Annotations are attributes from the System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations namespace applied to model/ViewModel properties to declare validation rules and display metadata. ASP.NET Core MVC enforces them during model binding and populates ModelState.

Common validation annotations:

public class RegisterViewModel
{
    [Required(ErrorMessage = "Name is required")]
    [StringLength(100, MinimumLength = 2)]
    [Display(Name = "Full Name")]
    public string Name { get; set; }

    [Required]
    [EmailAddress(ErrorMessage = "Invalid email format")]
    public string Email { get; set; }

    [Required]
    [DataType(DataType.Password)]
    [StringLength(50, MinimumLength = 8, ErrorMessage = "Password must be 8-50 chars")]
    [RegularExpression(@"^(?=.*[A-Z])(?=.*\d).+$",
        ErrorMessage = "Must contain uppercase and digit")]
    public string Password { get; set; }

    [Compare("Password", ErrorMessage = "Passwords do not match")]
    [DataType(DataType.Password)]
    public string ConfirmPassword { get; set; }

    [Range(18, 120, ErrorMessage = "Age must be between 18 and 120")]
    public int Age { get; set; }

    [Url]
    public string Website { get; set; }
}

Checking ModelState in the controller:

[HttpPost]
public async Task<IActionResult> Register(RegisterViewModel model)
{
    if (!ModelState.IsValid) // validation runs automatically during model binding
        return View(model);  // return form with error messages

    await _userService.CreateAsync(model);
    return RedirectToAction("Success");
}

Client-side validation:

Add jQuery Validation scripts and the unobtrusive validation script. ASP.NET Core generates data-val-* HTML attributes from annotations, which jQuery Validate reads to validate in the browser without a server round-trip.

followUps.question: How do you create a custom validation attribute?
followUps.answer:
Inherit from ValidationAttribute and override IsValid(object value, ValidationContext context):

public class NoPastDateAttribute : ValidationAttribute
{
    protected override ValidationResult IsValid(object value, ValidationContext ctx)
    {
        if (value is DateTime date && date.Date < DateTime.Today)
            return new ValidationResult("Date cannot be in the past");
        return ValidationResult.Success;
    }
}

// Usage
[NoPastDate]
public DateTime EventDate { get; set; }

For complex cross-property validation, implement IValidatableObject on the model class — the Validate() method receives the full object and can return multiple ValidationResults.

questions.tip: Server + client validation: Data annotations give you both. The server always re-validates (don't trust the client). The client-side validation (jQuery Validate) just reduces round-trips for common errors. Never skip server-side validation.

questions.question: What is the difference between TempData.Keep() and TempData.Peek() in ASP.NET Core MVC?
questions.answer:
TempData survives exactly one redirect by default — once you read a key, it's marked for deletion and gone on the next request. Keep() and Peek() are two ways to work around that deletion behaviour.

Default TempData behaviour:

// Action 1 — POST, sets TempData
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Save(OrderDto dto)
{
    TempData["Message"] = "Order saved!";
    return RedirectToAction("Confirm");
}

// Action 2 — Confirm reads it, TempData["Message"] is now MARKED for deletion
public IActionResult Confirm()
{
    var msg = TempData["Message"]; // reads it -- marked for deletion
    return View();
} // TempData["Message"] is gone after this response

// Action 3 — if you redirect again from Confirm, TempData["Message"] is null

TempData.Keep() — re-mark for survival

// Keep the value alive for one MORE request after reading
public IActionResult Confirm()
{
    var msg = TempData["Message"]; // reads it
    TempData.Keep("Message");      // keeps it alive for next request too
    // or TempData.Keep() -- keeps ALL tempdata keys
    return RedirectToAction("FinalStep");
}

public IActionResult FinalStep()
{
    var msg = TempData["Message"]; // still available because Keep() was called
    // now it's gone again (unless you call Keep() again)
}

TempData.Peek() — read without marking for deletion

public IActionResult Confirm()
{
    // Peek reads the value WITHOUT marking it for deletion
    // TempData["Message"] stays alive for the next request automatically
    var msg = TempData.Peek("Message") as string;
    return View(); // Message still available on next request
}
// vs
public IActionResult Confirm()
{
    var msg = TempData["Message"] as string; // reads + marks for deletion
    return View(); // Message gone after this
}

In Razor views:

<!-- Reading TempData in a view also marks it for deletion -->
@if (TempData["Message"] != null)
{
    <div class="alert alert-success">@TempData["Message"]</div>
}

<!-- To keep it alive from the view too -->
@{ TempData.Keep("Message"); }

Quick reference:

  • TempData[key] — reads, marks for deletion after current request.
  • TempData.Peek(key) — reads, does NOT mark for deletion, stays for next request.
  • TempData.Keep(key) — after reading, re-marks it to survive one more request.
  • TempData.Keep() — keeps ALL keys alive.

followUps.question: How is TempData stored internally? Is it always a cookie?
followUps.answer:
By default in ASP.NET Core, TempData is backed by the cookie-based provider — the data is serialised to JSON, Base64-encoded, and stored in a cookie. This means TempData survives even if the user's request hits a different server (unlike session, which is server-side by default). The cookie size limit applies — TempData shouldn't store large objects.

You can switch to session-based TempData:

builder.Services.AddControllersWithViews()
    .AddSessionStateTempDataProvider(); // use session instead of cookie

// Must also configure session
builder.Services.AddSession();
app.UseSession();

Session-based TempData is better for larger data, but adds server-side session infrastructure. Cookie-based is simpler and works out of the box for small messages like success/error notifications.

questions.tip: Pattern I use: Peek() when I need to read TempData in a layout/partial that runs on the same view as the main read. Keep() when I redirect again and need the value for one more hop. For most success/error toast messages, plain TempData[key] is enough.

questions.question: What is a strongly typed view in ASP.NET Core MVC? Why is it better than ViewBag?
questions.answer:
A strongly typed view explicitly declares the model type it expects using @model. The view then gets full IntelliSense, compile-time type checking, and no casting. I learned to always use strongly typed views after spending 30 minutes debugging a typo in a ViewBag key that the compiler could have caught instantly.

Weakly typed (ViewBag) — no compile-time safety:

// Controller
public IActionResult ProductDetail(int id)
{
    ViewBag.ProductName = "Widget Pro";
    ViewBag.Price = 49.99m;
    ViewBag.CategoryName = "Electronics";
    return View();
}
<!-- View — no @model declaration, no IntelliSense -->
<h1>@ViewBag.ProductNmae</h1> <!-- typo! No compile error. Blank at runtime. -->
<p>Price: @((decimal)ViewBag.Price):C</p> <!-- must cast -->

Strongly typed view — compile-time safety:

// ViewModel
public class ProductDetailViewModel
{
    public string Name { get; set; }
    public decimal Price { get; set; }
    public string CategoryName { get; set; }
    public bool InStock { get; set; }
    public List<string> Tags { get; set; }
}

// Controller
public IActionResult ProductDetail(int id)
{
    var product = _db.Products.Find(id);
    var vm = new ProductDetailViewModel
    {
        Name         = product.Name,
        Price        = product.Price,
        CategoryName = product.Category.Name,
        InStock      = product.Stock > 0
    };
    return View(vm);
}
<!-- View — @model declares the expected type -->
@model ProductDetailViewModel

<h1>@Model.Name</h1>           <!-- IntelliSense works, typos caught at compile time -->
<p>@Model.Price.ToString("C")</p> <!-- no cast needed, it's already decimal -->

@if (Model.InStock)
{
    <span class="badge-success">In Stock</span>
}
@foreach (var tag in Model.Tags)
{
    <span class="tag">@tag</span>
}

Advantages of strongly typed views:

  • Compile-time errors — typos in property names fail at build, not at runtime in front of users.
  • IntelliSense — IDE autocompletes @Model. with all available properties.
  • Refactoring — renaming a ViewModel property updates all references. Renaming a ViewBag key doesn't.
  • Tag Helpers — asp-for="Name" only works with strongly typed models, not ViewBag.
  • Validation — asp-validation-for="Email" reads Data Annotations from the model.

followUps.question: How do you pass a strongly typed model to a partial view?
followUps.answer:
<!-- Pass explicit model to partial -->
@await Html.PartialAsync("_ProductCard", Model.FeaturedProduct)

<!-- Tag helper approach (preferred) -->
<partial name="_ProductCard" model="Model.FeaturedProduct" />

<!-- Partial view declares its own model type -->
<!-- _ProductCard.cshtml -->
@model ProductCardViewModel
<div class="card">
    <h3>@Model.Name</h3>
    <p>@Model.Price.ToString("C")</p>
</div>

Each partial view can declare its own model type independently. If you don't pass a model, the partial inherits the parent view's model.

questions.tip: Golden rule: If a view needs more than 1-2 pieces of data, create a ViewModel. ViewModel naming convention: ViewName + 'ViewModel' (e.g., ProductDetailViewModel for the Detail view). Never expose your EF Core entities directly to views.

questions.question: What are Razor Pages in ASP.NET Core? How are they different from MVC?
questions.answer:
Razor Pages is an alternative to MVC for building page-focused web UIs in ASP.NET Core. Instead of splitting concerns across a Controller, Action, and View, Razor Pages co-locates the page's UI (the .cshtml file) and its handler code (the .cshtml.cs PageModel file) together. I find it cleaner for CRUD-style pages where you don't need the full Controller abstraction.

MVC structure:

// Separate files in separate folders
// Controllers/ProductsController.cs
public class ProductsController : Controller
{
    public IActionResult Index() => View(_db.Products.ToList());

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Create(ProductDto dto) { ... }
}
// Views/Products/Index.cshtml  <-- separate folder structure

Razor Pages structure — one page, one folder:

// Pages/Products/Index.cshtml.cs  <-- PageModel (handler)
public class IndexModel : PageModel
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public List<Product> Products { get; set; }

    [BindProperty]                     // auto-binds on POST
    public ProductDto NewProduct { get; set; }

    public IndexModel(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;

    // Handles GET /Products
    public void OnGet()
    {
        Products = _db.Products.ToList();
    }

    // Handles POST /Products
    public IActionResult OnPost()
    {
        if (!ModelState.IsValid) return Page();
        _db.Products.Add(new Product { Name = NewProduct.Name });
        _db.SaveChanges();
        return RedirectToPage();
    }

    // Named handler -- called by asp-page-handler="Delete"
    public IActionResult OnPostDelete(int id)
    {
        var p = _db.Products.Find(id);
        if (p != null) { _db.Products.Remove(p); _db.SaveChanges(); }
        return RedirectToPage();
    }
}
<!-- Pages/Products/Index.cshtml -->
@page
@model IndexModel

<form method="post">
    <input asp-for="NewProduct.Name" />
    <button type="submit">Add</button>
</form>

@foreach (var p in Model.Products)
{
    <form method="post" asp-page-handler="Delete">
        <span>@p.Name</span>
        <input type="hidden" name="id" value="@p.Id" />
        <button>Delete</button>
    </form>
}

MVC vs Razor Pages — when to use each:

  • Razor Pages — page-centric CRUD, admin panels, forms, simple websites. Less ceremony, file-per-page organization feels natural. The default Microsoft template for new web apps is Razor Pages.
  • MVC — complex routing logic, multiple views sharing one controller, API + view hybrid, when you need full controller flexibility, team familiar with MVC.

Both can coexist in the same app. I've run projects with Razor Pages for admin UI and MVC (or minimal API) for the API layer.

followUps.question: What is the @page directive in Razor Pages?
followUps.answer:
The @page directive at the top of a .cshtml file is what makes it a Razor Page rather than a regular MVC view. It tells ASP.NET Core to route directly to this file and handle GET/POST via the OnGet/OnPost handlers in the PageModel. Without @page, it's just a regular view that needs a controller to render it.

@page "{id:int}" adds a route template — /Products/Detail/5 maps to Detail.cshtml and the id is bound to the handler parameter.

questions.tip: Razor Pages is the Microsoft recommendation for new server-rendered apps. The ASP.NET Core team uses it for the Identity UI scaffolding. If you're building an API, use minimal APIs or controllers. If you're building pages, Razor Pages is often cleaner than MVC.

questions.question: What is the folder structure of an ASP.NET Core MVC project?
questions.answer:
Understanding the folder structure is often the first question in a junior-to-mid interview — it shows you understand how the framework is organized before diving into code.

MyMvcApp/
├── Controllers/              # Controller classes
│   ├── HomeController.cs     # Default controller
│   └── ProductsController.cs
│
├── Models/                   # Domain/entity models (EF Core entities)
│   └── Product.cs
│
├── ViewModels/               # View-specific models (not in default template but best practice)
│   └── ProductDetailViewModel.cs
│
├── Views/                    # Razor view files (.cshtml)
│   ├── Home/
│   │   ├── Index.cshtml
│   │   └── Privacy.cshtml
│   ├── Products/
│   │   ├── Index.cshtml
│   │   └── Detail.cshtml
│   └── Shared/
│       ├── _Layout.cshtml        # Master layout
│       ├── _ValidationScripts.cshtml
│       └── Error.cshtml
│
├── wwwroot/                  # Static files (CSS, JS, images) — served as-is
│   ├── css/
│   │   └── site.css
│   ├── js/
│   │   └── site.js
│   └── lib/                  # Client libraries (Bootstrap, jQuery)
│
├── Data/                     # EF Core DbContext, migrations
│   ├── AppDbContext.cs
│   └── Migrations/
│
├── Services/                 # Business logic services (not in default template)
│   └── IProductService.cs
│
├── Properties/
│   └── launchSettings.json   # Dev launch profiles (ports, env vars)
│
├── appsettings.json          # App configuration (connection strings etc.)
├── appsettings.Development.json  # Dev-specific overrides
└── Program.cs                # Entry point (replaces Startup.cs in .NET 6+)

Key files explained:

  • Program.cs — app entry point, service registration, middleware pipeline.
  • appsettings.json — configuration (DB connections, API keys, feature flags). Never commit secrets here.
  • Views/Shared/_Layout.cshtml — the HTML shell that wraps every page (nav, footer, CSS/JS imports).
  • Views/_ViewStart.cshtml — sets default layout for all views.
  • Views/_ViewImports.cshtml — global @using and @addTagHelper for all views.
  • wwwroot/ — the only folder directly accessible from the browser. Everything else is server-side only.

Convention over configuration:

MVC finds views by convention: ProductsController.Index() looks for Views/Products/Index.cshtml by default. You never configure this mapping — it's automatic.

followUps.question: Why is wwwroot separate from other folders?
followUps.answer: ASP.NET Core's static files middleware only serves content from wwwroot/. Files outside wwwroot/ are never directly accessible from a browser — you can't visit /Controllers/HomeController.cs or /appsettings.json. This is a deliberate security boundary. Without app.UseStaticFiles(), even wwwroot files won't be served. In production, you can point your CDN or reverse proxy (Nginx, IIS) directly to the wwwroot folder for maximum performance, bypassing the ASP.NET Core process entirely for static assets.

questions.tip: Add a ViewModels/ folder immediately. The default template doesn't create one, but you should. Putting ViewModels directly in Models/ mixes domain models and view-specific models. A separate folder makes the distinction clear and helps new team members navigate the project.

questions.question: What are filters in ASP.NET Core MVC? Explain the different types.
questions.answer:
Filters are attributes or middleware-like components that run at specific points in the MVC request pipeline — before or after controller actions, results, or exception handling. They're perfect for cross-cutting concerns like logging, authorization, validation, and caching.

Five filter types, in execution order:

  1. Authorization Filters — run first. Decide if the request is allowed. [Authorize] is built-in. Short-circuit before anything else runs.
  2. Resource Filters — run after auth, before model binding. Good for caching the entire response.
  3. Action Filters — run before and after the action method executes. Most commonly used: logging, validation, modifying action arguments.
  4. Exception Filters — handle unhandled exceptions thrown by actions or other filters. Good for formatting error responses.
  5. Result Filters — run before and after the IActionResult executes (after action, before response writes). Good for response transformation.
// Custom Action Filter — logs timing for every action
public class TimingFilter : IActionFilter
{
    private readonly ILogger<TimingFilter> _logger;
    private Stopwatch _sw;

    public TimingFilter(ILogger<TimingFilter> logger) => _logger = logger;

    public void OnActionExecuting(ActionExecutingContext context)
    {
        _sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();
    }

    public void OnActionExecuted(ActionExecutedContext context)
    {
        _sw.Stop();
        _logger.LogInformation("{Action} took {Ms}ms",
            context.ActionDescriptor.DisplayName, _sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);
    }
}

// Custom Exception Filter — returns JSON error for API controllers
public class ApiExceptionFilter : IExceptionFilter
{
    public void OnException(ExceptionContext context)
    {
        context.Result = new ObjectResult(new { error = context.Exception.Message })
        {
            StatusCode = 500
        };
        context.ExceptionHandled = true;
    }
}

// Register globally in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddControllersWithViews(options =>
{
    options.Filters.Add<TimingFilter>();
});

// Or per-controller/action using attribute
[ServiceFilter(typeof(TimingFilter))]
public class ProductsController : Controller { }

// Built-in examples
[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]   // Authorization filter
[ValidateAntiForgeryToken]      // Action filter
[ResponseCache(Duration = 60)] // Result filter

Filters vs Middleware: Middleware runs for ALL requests (including static files). Filters only run within the MVC pipeline and have access to MVC context (action name, controller, model state). Use middleware for app-wide concerns, filters for MVC-specific ones.
followUps: What is the difference between an Exception Filter and UseExceptionHandler middleware?
How do you pass dependencies into filters (ServiceFilter vs TypeFilter)?
What is IAsyncActionFilter and when do you use it over IActionFilter?
questions.tip: Execution order: Authorization → Resource → Action (before) → Action runs → Action (after) → Exception → Result. Filters have MVC context; middleware doesn't. Use ServiceFilter<T> to inject dependencies into attribute-based filters.

questions.question: How do you handle exceptions globally in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Global exception handling means you catch unhandled exceptions in one place rather than wrapping every action in try/catch. ASP.NET Core gives you several options depending on whether you need JSON responses (API) or redirect to error pages (MVC).

Option 1: UseExceptionHandler middleware — the built-in approach for most apps:

// Program.cs — different behavior per environment
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage(); // detailed stack trace
}
else
{
    // MVC apps: redirect to /Home/Error
    app.UseExceptionHandler("/Home/Error");
    app.UseHsts();
}

// For APIs: return RFC 7807 ProblemDetails JSON
app.UseExceptionHandler(errApp =>
{
    errApp.Run(async context =>
    {
        var ex = context.Features.Get<IExceptionHandlerFeature>()?.Error;
        context.Response.StatusCode = ex is NotFoundException ? 404 : 500;
        context.Response.ContentType = "application/problem+json";

        var problem = new ProblemDetails
        {
            Title = "An error occurred",
            Status = context.Response.StatusCode,
            Detail = ex?.Message
        };
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(problem);
    });
});

Option 2: IExceptionHandler interface (.NET 8+) — clean, injectable, supports DI:

public class GlobalExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
{
    private readonly ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> _logger;
    public GlobalExceptionHandler(ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> logger) => _logger = logger;

    public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
        HttpContext context, Exception ex, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        _logger.LogError(ex, "Unhandled exception: {Message}", ex.Message);

        var statusCode = ex switch
        {
            NotFoundException => StatusCodes.Status404NotFound,
            UnauthorizedAccessException => StatusCodes.Status403Forbidden,
            _ => StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError
        };

        context.Response.StatusCode = statusCode;
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new
        {
            error = ex.Message,
            traceId = Activity.Current?.Id ?? context.TraceIdentifier
        }, ct);

        return true; // true = handled, don't propagate
    }
}

// Register in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<GlobalExceptionHandler>();
builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();
app.UseExceptionHandler(); // no path needed with IExceptionHandler

Option 3: Exception Filter — for MVC/API action-level. Doesn't catch middleware exceptions or background tasks. Good for API-specific error formatting in a controller context.

In production I use the IExceptionHandler (.NET 8) approach — it's DI-friendly, keeps exception handling logic organized, and pairs nicely with Serilog for structured logging including the TraceId.
followUps: What is ProblemDetails and why should you return it from API error responses?
How do you handle exceptions in background services or IHostedService?
What is the difference between UseExceptionHandler and exception filters in MVC?
questions.tip: .NET 8+: use IExceptionHandler for DI-friendly global handling. Before .NET 8: UseExceptionHandler(path) for MVC, inline lambda for APIs. Always include TraceId in error responses so you can correlate logs.
